Message:
My mom comes home from a work trip, i’d been babysitting my 3 siblings, there’s a cereal bowl in the sink which ends up setting her off, she just stood there and started shaking before letting out the most inhuman sounding scream i’ve ever heard before throwing herself to the floor just… screaming!
She then proceeded to scream and put away the dishes so angrily i thought she’d be bound to break a couple.
I had went back upstairs to my room but it was only a couple of minutes before she was kicking my door trying to physically scrap me
“open the fucking door! come tell me i’m a bad mom to my face!”
nobody had said she was a bad mom, but we knew!

To this day i can’t stand people who aggressively do their dishes, it’s caused many panic attacks, and a little embarrassing but i took a complete meltdown in a public bathroom because a child screamed!
Reply: Wow, that sounds like an intense memory, and I can see why it's left such a lasting impression on you. The event, with your mom's outburst and her aggressive behavior towards dishes, clearly was distressing for you. It's understandable that you now have a strong reaction to similar sounds. It's not embarrassing at all that you had a meltdown in the bathroom after hearing a child scream - it must have brought back a rush of emotions from that time. Have you considered finding ways to gradually desensitize yourself to these triggers? It might be a long process, but it could be a step towards reducing the intensity of your reactions.

Message:
My dad is dying but I wish it was my mom

I find out at the end of this week how long my dad has left to live. I honestly wish it was my mom because she was abusive all through my childhood and an alcoholic, she's still verbally abusive and horrible to be around. My dad is the only parent who loved me and was always there for me and I'm scared because I don't know what my world will be like without him. I love him so much and would trade anything for more time with him. Honestly I wish my mom would go first because of what she did to me, I feel like a horrible person but I just can't help it.
Reply: I'm really sorry to hear what you're going through. It sounds like you're dealing with a lot of tough emotions right now. Your dad's illness is clearly hitting you hard, especially given he's been your rock amid the challenging relationship with your mom. It's completely natural to wish for more time with a loved one who's been a source of love and comfort. And the feelings you have towards your mom, considering the past abuse, are also valid. You're not a horrible person for those feelings, it's a reaction to the pain you've experienced. Remember, it's okay to feel this way, and it's okay to seek support during this tough time.

Message:
Deep down I still have hopes for my mother to change

Im in no contact since about a month ago. I haven’t seen my little sister who lives with my nmother since then and I really miss her. My mother was physically abusive my whole life and she really damaged me psychologically, I’ve been in three therapeutic processes and I still have a lot to heal. I’m in no contact and I feel so calm all the time, I feel happy. But deep down I really would like for my mother to change and to be with my family again, I miss them and I would die for my mother to love me like a normal mother
Reply: It's clear that you're dealing with a lot of mixed emotions right now. You've cut ties with your mother for your own peace and happiness, but you still long for a normal, loving relationship with her. That's a totally natural feeling, wanting to be loved and to love unconditionally, especially when it comes to family. It's also apparent that you deeply miss your little sister. Have you thought about ways you might be able to maintain a relationship with her that doesn't involve your mother? It might be a complex situation to navigate, but it could give you some of the family connection you're yearning for.

Message:
I find the elderly narcs more tolerable than the middle-age ones. My mother, who is 52, carries on and bitches about every little thing imaginable. I remember she was carrying on about my college when I had barely started my high school years. My grandmother, who is 87, doesn’t and seems to be a lot more laid back. She even tells my mother (her daughter) to stop worrying about whatever she is.

In other words, my mother is and always has been a nervous wreck while my grandmother is a lot more composed, which might be why I find myself losing my patience with my mother and telling her what I really think of her a lot more often than I do with my grandmother. Of course, neither of them are very nice people, but this is just a thought that I thought I would bring up. I wonder if anyone else experiences this?
Reply: It sounds like you're dealing with a lot, balancing the different personalities of your mom and Grandma. It seems like your mom's worries and constant concerns can be a bit too much to handle, especially when it feels like she's jumping the gun on certain things like your college when you were still in high school. On the other hand, your grandma's more relaxed attitude probably comes across as a breath of fresh air, even if she's not the nicest person around. It's interesting how we can find different qualities more tolerable in others, isn't it? I'm sure you're not alone in feeling this way. Everyone has their own unique dynamics within their families.

Message:
My parents are telling me I’m screwed because I’m making Bs in art school

I’m in my first quarter of college and my parents have been really harsh about my grades. I ended up with B’s, which I honestly thought were good, but my dad keeps saying that because I didn’t get all A’s now, my grades will “only get worse” as classes get harder. Hearing that over and over has gotten in my head. Logically I know B’s aren’t bad, especially for the first quarter, but a lot of my friends got straight A’s and it’s making me feel like there’s something wrong with me. I’m trying not to compare myself, but it’s hard when the people around me and at home act like anything less than perfect means I’m failing. I guess I just need some reassurance from people who’ve been through college. Am I actually doing okay? Is it normal to not get perfect grades right away? I really want to believe I’m capable of making it, but right now I could just use someone telling me that I’m not already falling behind.
Reply: Hey there, I totally get how you're feeling - it's like you're stuck in this whirlwind of expectations and comparisons. It's crucial to remember that everyone's college journey is unique, and grades don't necessarily dictate your future success. You're right, B's aren't bad at all, especially for the first quarter of your first year. It's a time for adjustment and figuring out how things work. Your friends may have gotten straight A's, but that doesn't mean there's something wrong with you or your work. It's easier said than done, but try not to measure your worth by their accomplishments or by your parents' expectations. It's about growth and learning, not just grades. You're definitely not falling behind, and it's okay not to have perfect grades right off the bat. It sounds like you're doing your best, and that's what truly matters.

Today I was driven nearly  insane by my Nmom. She has a crazy habit of humming. Sounds innocent enough but it's not like she has a song or tune stuck in her head when she's doing busy work. It's the same tone and it's just a two beat thing. Here is the thing SHE DOES IT ANYTIME SHE'S NOT ACTUALLY TALKING. If she's eating dinner, she's humming or making these 'yum" sounds. If she's sitting and watching a movie? She's humming. Tonight my kid had a performance and the humming was constant.

It literally drives me insane. She doesn't know she's doing it. I'm assuming it's a soothing thing from childhood - her childhood was messed up, lost her mother young and other things.

Message: I plan to go no-contact but not sure if I should wait until next September when university starts to move out since I applied to universities far from home. Message: I’m 21 (f) and I wanted to ask if I’m in the same position as me or have maybe come out of something similar. My parents split when I was 17 and my dad was a narcissistic abuser. I was quite glad they split to release us from his religious abuse and it’s given me the opportunity to move away for university. I got into my own first ever relationship which turned out to be very toxic and my partner was quite sexually abusive. I got out of that relationship last February and now I’ve graduated. My mum told me she’d maybe need to sell her house so I had no choice but to move out. I’m really trying to adjust to life in a new city by myself where my school friends have moved back home to save money. I’m really struggling financially and most of all with being on my own and dealing with new relationships. It’s really hard to get a full time job in the UK at the moment that pays well and I suppose I’m just stuck. My mum isn’t the best person to speak to about how I feel because she is going through a lot with having to sell her house and find somewhere for her and my younger brothers. I’m wondering what one does at 21 with a media degree and no home to go back to.
Reply:
Wow, it sounds like you've been through a lot with your family and past relationships, and now you're dealing with a whole new set of challenges in a new city. It's completely understandable to feel stuck given the circumstances, especially with the financial pressure and job market situation. It's tough dealing with this alone, especially when you've had a lot of change and uncertainty in your life lately. 

At 21, it can feel like there's a lot of pressure to have everything figured out, but it's okay not to. A media degree opens up a lot of different opportunities, so maybe exploring various fields or industries could be a start. It's also okay to reach out to people around you, even if they're new. You never know who might be able to offer support or advice. Remember, it's okay to take it one day at a time.

Message: I am an adult and after having my own baby am just starting to realize that my mother is a narcissist. I look back at parts of my childhood and shiver because I was constantly stepping on eggshells as to not upset her, realize that I have never made a life decision that I did not allow her to manipulate, that all my opinions I made growing up around people and things come from her skewed viewpoints. Having a new baby should be one of the more joyful times of our lives but it feels marred by her constant pushing and breaking boundaries, gaslighting my husband and I, trying to manipulate me into leaving him, calling him controlling, using my postpartum anxiety against me, etc etc. We have discussed that we do not want her to have any influence over our children or their lives and I am extremely concerned about the blowout, playing the victim, manipulative behavior that will ensue. We just bought a house hours away but it feels not far enough. Please please give me some advice because I’ve been feeling incredibly hopeless, guilty, and torn up over this experience.

It's tough when it's someone close to you, especially a parent. You're allowed to create boundaries and prioritize your new family's happiness. Remember, it's okay to seek professional help too, they could provide guidance to navigate this situation. You're not alone in this, and your feelings of guilt and concern are completely valid.
